speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four, interview with Selma Engel. You were telling us about the difficulties of being clean in Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had scabies. The, the lice, I thought it was the worst. But it was not the worst. It didn't-- I mean, you get used to it. You, you looked every evening, before you went to bed. Chaim helped me, always looking, all the bed bugs and things like that, to take out. And we-- when we-- see-- when we got into sorting clothes, there was a lot of food in. And you was not allowed to take anything. When you take something-- once a boy opened a can of sardine, and they shoot him half dead. And they put him on a door-- it looked like a door. And a few from the kapos had to hold him. And we have to see how half dead he was, and that, that he-- that will happen to us, when we eat anything for the food that comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you're hungry, you do it anyway. So I was very good in that. I was a pretty heavy girl. And I put in my bra, in my underpants, food, and took it with me to where we slept. And, and we shared it. We shared it with four or five girls. So those were the-- what was the question that you asked me? Well, you were telling us about stealing the food, how dangerous it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. So, yeah. So it was very dangerous. And I really didn't see the danger, I think. I didn't see the danger, because I was never near to death that I-- you know-- that-- and I tell you, you put away from you. Because when it didn't come a transport, two weeks, we know that then they will put us in the gas chamber. So you know the transports will come, anyway. So you hope they come to Sobibor. Anyway, you hope that came transport. Came not only Dutch transport, they came transport from Italy, and from France, and from Poland. And the Polish Jews, they didn't want to go to the gas chamber, most of them. And they had loud screaming. And we were locked up in our-- where we slept. And we were locked up. And you hear screaming and shooting, very bad. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, once was it, was an uprising in Belzec. And they all came to Sobibor. And they wouldn't go to the gas chamber. So as a result, they shoot them all to death. And the next day, Chaim found a letter in his pocket. And it said, take [NON-ENGLISH], take, take [NON-ENGLISH]. Take [NON-ENGLISH]. I forgot the English word. That you should take-- it's to kill, kill them. Do something against them, you know? Revenge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Revenge. Take revenge. Was written in a little book, pants was full with blood and everything. He will tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are-- always lived in fear. Always, we lived in fear, all the time. And we, we-- it was in danger from every minute of the day. Once a cat walked-- or a dog walked up the-- or something, animal, walked on the fence with us. And it went off. And we had to stay a whole night up, roll call.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had, always, some other punishment for. We had to walk around, like and sing-- singing always [INAUDIBLE] when I was there. Walked for hours, and hours, and hours, singing German songs. Or we had to lay down on the floor and going up. Hold-- when we walked around. Especially the Dutch people, Dutch men-- not the women, so much. But the Dutch men had to do-- for punishment, punishment, they look for punishment-- work. And they had to-- were not allowed to go sit one minute. They had to jog all the time, and run all the time, and do things until they drop dead. Do you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course. That I saw going on. And I say, oh-- and sometimes I know them. And also, a lot of Dutch people came there. And most intellectuals, [INAUDIBLE] that came in from universities, but never went through anything. And then most of the time, the wife was already in the gas chamber. But they found out. And they laid down, and they died just when-- they died, sometimes in a few days. They didn't want to eat, and they died, because they couldn't comprehend what was going on, there. And in between, Chaim and I fell in love.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we fell-- I fell in love with Chaim. I, I, I think so. And we were young. And that we had each other. We stayed together so much as we worked together. And we stayed together a lot. We were-- the Germans called us, already, [GERMAN] because we always were together. And, and I think that saved-- yeah, that saved our-- that saved our sanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: It saved your sanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. Because we could help each other, you know? And also, we-- I found a, a second cousin in there. And she had-- and a girl that I know, too. So we were like a little family in camp. And we separated us, really, from the-- all the other girls, together. We really separated ourselves. I, I, I was very good, what I told you, stealing food and things like that. And then we shared it. And sometimes, when there was not a transport, I had to go in the woods and, and pick mushrooms for the Germans. And so we have-- we could make what little-- what people go camping with. And we found it, all in the Dutch things. And, and we cooked it very careful, in the-- that nobody knows about it. And so we shared things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- as came a transport-- not hungry. Because we tried to steal that food, because the food, what we-- people would have to live on it. And a lot of Dutch people what didn't have a chance, and didn't work there, sorting. Because just a few people would-- worked by the sorting. And what they had to work for very hard labor, cutting trees, and making wells, and things like that. They didn't get the food. And they were very undernourished, and very, very, very sick. So that's the reason that, that we, in the time-- that we hope, always, that there came a transport. That we had to eat, and that we could live. The food, what we got, you couldn't live on. We got a piece of bread, but was sour. It was like clay. You couldn't eat it. I remember that when I eat it, I had a little bit-- piece of garlic. And I could put over it. And then I could eat it, a little bit. And the water what you got was brown water, was coffee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we got-- in the afternoon, we got soup. They made it from meat they-- meat. We never showed the meat. I don't know what [INAUDIBLE] meat. And was made from the outside [INAUDIBLE] from straw or something like that. You couldn't eat it. And then the Dutch people, what were so hungry, were standing next to you. And, and they wanted it. And sometimes, I give it. Chaim was always mad at me that I did that. Because you have to eat, too. But you know, it was just-- they used-- when you're hungry, you throw your dignity away. You have nothing left anymore. You know, when you're hungry, and you're dirty, and you live in such a situation, like-- in, in, in, in camp like that, where you're not nothing-- you're absolutely not even a number. So the Dutch people, they couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So also, was one day, what happened that-- and there was one-- there were 72 Dutch Jews there. Of course, they were only a Jewish camp. 72 Dutch men, all young men-- most of them. And there was one German Jew what went to the Germans and told what, we hate this guy. We hate that guy. We'll kill him, or something like that. He went to the Germans, and told what we were talking about it. So he want also that we get numbers. And one of the kapos was friendly with one of the Germans-- [? Francil ?] was his name. And he say, [? Francil ?] say, you know what you do with a guy like that. We did it in the military. You put a sack over his head, and you kill him. And I remember very well that I want to go and see what, how'd he kill him. Well, I know that was a dangerous guy, because he told the Germans that there were-- all the Dutch people want to run away. But they didn't-- it's impossible, because they know, in Poland, you cannot speak the language. You don't have-- where do you go? Where would they go? So they shot-- 72 Dutch men, got all shot. And I remember so vividly, one of them what I know very well-- Malpesee Hundelav was his name. He came from [INAUDIBLE]. And he gave me a little piece of bread-- butter. And he say, when I come back, will you give it to me? So you never came back. And they were all shot, because of the German. So they did what the German say. They put a sack over him, and they killed him. Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapos and the Jews, themselves, in the camp, because he was a spy, on the [INAUDIBLE]. And he was a danger-- he thought he will be holier than the Pope, Chaim, my husband, always say. Because he went over there, and told everything what was going on in, in, in camp. So-- And I remember that I want to go and see. And Chaim said, you don't go. You don't go. I didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- so it's little-- so-- but life was a hell. But because Chaim and I, we met each other, and we had each other, I think that made it a little bit softer for both of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one of the girls what-- what was in love with Chaim. And before I came, he had-- he had a lunch with her or some-- in the evening, something to eat with her. And she was very jealous. And she told the girls that I was a spy . I was already a few months, in camp. And one of the girls-- the Oolah Stern, what was my buddy, what they came together from Holland, she say, are you nuts. She was standing there. She was also standing. You crazy, she say. She say, you are, perhaps, the spy! When-- and-- when that girl wouldn't have spoke up for me, they would have killed me. She was just jealous that Chaim was with me. You know, things like that-- it was such an-- it was such a strange world. We didn't have much-- we didn't have much in common with the Polish people at all, because we didn't speak Yiddish. And they spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and Chaim talk to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think German. Because we all spoke German. And I think Chaim and I-- and they always say love is-- we didn't-- yeah, I think, German. I think German, I think. And then-- and all-- I was-- I had typhus. So I think Chaim saved my life, also, that he, he helped me, I think. With typhus, you don't know what to do. And I remember, also, I was once-- you cannot lay-- you cannot be sick. When you're sick in Sobibor, they right away bring you to-- you go to the gas chamber. They say, [GERMAN], and you see on-- they shoot you, right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once, I was in bed. And he say, who is sick? And he screamed in the woman barrack. And then he went to the men, and see the men all standing there. And I came out, and all-- and I know-- a lot of people from-- I know very well, from that I was young. They were all by the Zionist organization. So then I came out, and there was [? Francil ?] again, what took me out of [INAUDIBLE]. You go back to work. So I ran back to work. And I think, then, the girls put me-- hide me. I was hidden somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me that, later, that when I was so sick, I couldn't do anything. But you had to work. Chaim had typhus, and he worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Francil-- ?] was this [? Francil ?] some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Francil ?] liked me, somehow. Was a married man, but that doesn't say anything, you know? And that was-- that's dangerous, really. Because I remember I had, once, very heavy work. And he said, go here. Come here. Do that [INAUDIBLE] work. And I had to cut soap up for-- they made something out of that, later, in big pieces of-- so I had cut soap up, things like that. And that I remember, vaguely. And later, we talked about it and that. But I remember, [INAUDIBLE] in Germany, when I saw them all there, sitting, all the SSes, I was afraid to smile to him. So I didn't. I, I recognized him, but I remember, I didn't look. But I don't know, because you saw a familiar face, perhaps, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this at a trial, later?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in a trial, yeah. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other couples in the camp, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were more couples in the camp. And Chaim was the only man what took his girlfriend, [INAUDIBLE] while we ran away. He was the only-- all the other ones left the girls. There were-- a few were sick, and nobody else. And he was the only one what took his Dutch girlfriend. All the other Polish girls, they left them all there. Nobody, nobody, nobody took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I walked once, in the woods, and said-- one of the guys what had [INAUDIBLE] girl already, do you know so-and-so? I don't know, even, the name. And he say-- I say, no. You should know. I don't have to know. Nobody took with them. So-- So every day, you saw Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we work together in the-- sorting clothes. And when there was not a transport, I didn't see him. Once, he worked in a pit, to make a well. And that was very dangerous. I think they threw a pail down. And thank god it didn't kill him. Yeah, we worked together. And we saw each other every evening. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a day off?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there religious Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very religious Jews. The-- they know when it was Yom Kippur. And-- well, it was-- but it was not a religious like you hear from other camps, that there was a holiday, and things like that. But you could see they were praying. You know, you could see they were praying. But of course, there were very religious Jews there. Yeah. There were a lot of Polish Jews. The Dutch Jews were not so religious, but I know what came there, then, were the young. Most were young people that came there, in Sobibor. But there were-- yeah. Was very religious Jews were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody ever talk about how they felt about being religious, about God?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we didn't talk much with other people. Somehow, Chaim, perhaps. But we didn't talk much about-- you were so busy with everyday life that-- you know, I remember that I always looked up. And I know my mother was dead, and my brother was dead. So I talked always that way, like to the sky. I talked with them and I thought, you know, they are somewhere that I can talk to them. But no, you didn't talk much with each other. And we didn't have much contact with anybody else-- none almost. Because you, you, you came home, and you looked if you had lice. And if you could take them off, and then you had make-- prepare some food. And, and then was already dark , and you were dead tired. You, you, you went to bed, and you fell half-dead in bed and slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have much contact and conversations, not that I know, with Chaim and me, and, and the Dutch girls. No, we didn't have much. No, we didn't talk much about religion, of-- not that-- what I know. Perhaps they did, but not what I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had more freedom, too, to come to each other, the Polish Jews. Because they were almost all Polish Jews. And one had a tailor's shop, that he could go to the tailor. And they talked together. And they had-- one is in a shop for a gold smith. So they could talk together, a little bit more. And they could talk about different things with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you started to talk about the uprising. What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, what it was also, I remember that, that there came a lot of Jews from Minsk. Minsk was a ghetto, I think, and also a concentration camp. But it was a ghetto. And they came-- all came to Sobibor, the whole-- they liquidated that. The Germans were pulling up already a little bit. And they came all to Sobibor-- lots came to Sobibor. I remember, one girl was even in Holland, in the war, what I remember her, and that she came from Minsk, somehow. She came to Sobibor. And they built-- they built it, already, very big barracks, all over. The Jews had to build very big barracks. And the whole camp changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on the end time. There was--

speaker: interviewee
text: September, 1943. September of 1943. '43. Yeah, September 1943. So-- and then came also, from Minsk-- the, the, the, the, military Jews came. But I didn't know. I didn't have no idea. We didn't have any connections with them. I don't even remember where they slept. But I think they slept in the other barracks. And then, also, the transport starts slowly not coming anymore. So, so there was-- so we didn't-- so we worked hard-- they worked hard on the barracks. And I-- we had to-- they looked for work. Like we had to clean in the woods, for leaves. You know, they looked for work, for us. And it was very heavy work, what we had to do. Trees, moving from one to the other, and when it was not a transport. And we had to-- like big trolleys-- there's a big trolley, which it can tip over. We had to fill with sand, and had to move it away. So we had very hard labor we had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't think so much about this work, but the Polish Jews. And so there was, already-- and when he came in, the-- what was his name? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Comes up. One of the Russian Jews, what made the uprising. He was the one main person what made uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came in, the first day, he want to run away. And they calmed him down. When he saw what was going on, that they're killing there and hitting them half to death-- and so he was the one what say he wants to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they calmed him down when they hear the story. And they talked with him. Like what-- like you see-- read in the book. And what they did about it, and also like you saw in the movie.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he-- they organized an already, what was-- what we did-- what I didn't know. I had typhus. In that time, was already, I was a little bit better. In, in September, I think, I was sick. And I don't remember much from that time. But one day, we stepped also in a new barrack. Also, a lot-- a lot of Dutch girls were sleeping there. And they were also sick. And he say to me that they-- from the uprising-- he say, Selma, put a lot of clothes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim. And come to that and that place. So I put a lot of clothes on. I went, even, and-- I don't know where I got it from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask him why?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Whenever-- he was not allowed to tell it. I ask him why, but he was not allowed to tell anything. But it was really dangerous, just-- and he didn't know anything about. They didn't have him involved, because of me. But I didn't trust the Dutch people, because didn't speak the language. And we, for the Polish Jews, they didn't think that we were really Jews, because we didn't speak Yiddish, and we didn't speak any of the lang-- we had a different kind of culture. So didn't want anything to do-- they didn't believe us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was not involved in it. But his friend what was in the fireplace, what worked-- burnt everything-- also, I forgot to tell you, when we sorted clothes, Chaim knows-- he thought, always, that one day he will run away. And also, to, to-- only thing to go against the Germans, then they took the gold, what was many times in the-- in the Polish, Dutch Jews, hidden all over. And they took so much as possible, as they come. And he put it in that bag, what-- in the suit case. And that went to the fireplace. And then the fire friend of him buried it in the ground. So when we ran away, they divided that. So that was very important, for without money, we wouldn't have saved our life, either. So Chaim said to me, put a lot of-- and I didn't know anything about it, because I didn't think about money. I, I-- it was not much very-- I didn't even-- I know I will die here, and that-- I did-- I don't know. I didn't even think we're ever running away. So when Chaim told me, put a lot of clothes on, so-- and be at 4:00 in that and that place, I put a lot of clothes on. And I'm lucky that the German didn't see me, when he would have said, what's she doing there, with so much clothes on? It was so ridiculous. So when I came there, Chaim was standing outside and with another guy. So we were, the three of us were standing there. I remember I went-- I was standing there a little bit longer. I went inside, and one of the kapos-- Vershuvski is his name-- came back. And he was very nervous. He just killed a German. And I give him some pill for the-- for to calm him down. And he-- I see him spit it out. I don't need that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went-- he killed the most-- he was a kapo, but he was very good. Most kapos were good in Sobibor, really. And they-- he killed three Germans, all alone, by himself. And he came in the-- inside. And I walked with him, so inside, and then I went the other door, out. And Chaim was standing there, talking with that guy. And then, he-- the other guy was involved in the uprising. And he had to go to kill a German what was sitting-- had an office where we pass by when we at roll call. And he was sitting there, in that office. And when he knows that we are passing by, and it was a little bit not so organized, he would have start shooting. So that SS had to be killed. So he said to him, Chaim say, you have to go, now. Said, I don't want to go. So Chaim, well, you have to go. Are so many dead already. We don't know exactly how many. But there was one [INAUDIBLE], what is also a very important part in story from Sobibor. And he told everybody, when he was allowed to go around a little bit, how many was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Chaim knows already, there were 10 dead, or 12 dead, or 8 dead. There was no way out, anymore. That was it. And so [INAUDIBLE] you don't go. I have to go. And Chaim say that [INAUDIBLE] took a knife. But I don't remember. Anyway, he took just a regular bread knife, and he went there. And then at once, he was gone. I remember that so well. Chaim was gone. He took a knife.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took a knife, and he was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: With this other man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the other man didn't want to go. Chaim went instead of the other man.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other man didn't want to go. So then I went looking where Chaim was. And I hear from that office screaming like they kill a pig. Such screaming. And I-- then I know where Chaim was. So I went back, and I thought, better nobody sees me than I walk there. So I went quickly back to the barrack, where the house-- where there was an house where all the medicine and everything, they hide. That was there, and would lead us into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was waited. And then was the roll call, and I had no Chaim. And we had to go work at the roll call. And I thought, what will I do without Chaim? Where will I go? Ay-yi-yi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, Chaim came out of the office. All the blood, and his hand was bleeding. And I had to clean his hand, when I thought we were walking outside, perhaps people will see it. If he has blood on his face, I had no idea what will happen. So I clean him quickly, and put something around his arm, a wound from that-- that knife was just a bread knife, broke off, and hit in his pulse. And then, we start walking. And we went to Camp I, the entrance from Camp I. And everybody was standing there and said, hurrah! And there comes some Ukrainians on bicycles, and some people just kill them off, the Ukrainians. And then, the Ukrainians up the posts, where there was the big posts, all around--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Ukrainians who were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the guards. And they were standing on these big posts that's all around. And they starting shooting. And then, we start running. And then [? Francil, ?] what I told you, [INAUDIBLE] he had, I see him, I think, with the the top from a garbage pail in the front of him. And he start shooting with a machine gun. And then we start, all, running. And there was a little house. And everybody stopped. Chaim said, come, Selma. And then he takes my hand. And we running father. And then we, we go to the main entrance. We were lucky, when we have people-- the men going off on all sides. Later, I remember that. When you run, you just run, and run, and run. I remember, I had a necklace. When all the Jewish people what came, had all different Jewish things on-- [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, oh, now I come in public, I better-- I took it off, took that off and throw it away. But I thought, God forbid somebody sees me with that Jewish things on. And I done-- I run, I took off-- slowly, all my clothes I took off, because it was too hot. You could not run very fast with all the clothes were there on. And we run, and we run, and we run, until we come in the middle in the woods-- until the woods. No, and we came around farther in the woods. It start raining a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw somebody there, and he asked me where his girlfriend is. And I say, I know? You have to know. And then we come with a whole bunch of people-- from Sobibor we came, [INAUDIBLE]. And somehow, they-- we wanted to go with them. And they didn't want us, because I was Dutch. They didn't trust the Dutch people. So [INAUDIBLE] had a gun, because somebody stole from the Germans. And also, some had a chance. Like [INAUDIBLE] had a chance to come in the places where the Germans sit, and stole guns. And so some had these guns. And they want to shoot Chaim. Let's stop right here. We have to go to the next tape.
